core_takeaway: No first-position payoff advantage appears in this transcript. Agent_1 used the first speaking position to anchor Jewel, but Agent_3's last-position veto power blocked Round 1 when Agent_3 got nothing, and the eventual Round 2 consensus gave all agents equal final utility.
why_or_counterexample: The clearest counterexample is that the first formal proposal did not win unanimity: it protected Agent_1 and Agent_2 but excluded Agent_3, who rejected it. Consensus arrived only after the allocation gave Agent_1 Jewel, Agent_2 Quill+Stone, and Agent_3 Apple+Pencil; final utilities were equal at 90.0 each, so the earlier position did not beat the last position.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Public discussion quickly identified the compatible preference structure: Agent_1 wanted only Jewel, Agent_2 wanted Quill+Stone, and Agent_3 wanted Apple+Pencil. Round 1 discussion already described the balanced deal, but execution lagged until Round 2.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private thinking was available and consistently favored the same canonical allocation: keep Jewel with Agent_1 while splitting the non-Jewel top pairs between Agents 2 and 3. These entries show convergence rather than first-mover dominance.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Round 1 formal proposals failed to instantiate the public three-way bargain because they left Agent_3 empty or moved Jewel. Round 2 proposals from all three agents converged on the same allocation, removing any positional asymmetry.
phase_notes.private_voting: Round 1 voting shows Agent_1 and Agent_2 could accept proposals that excluded Agent_3, but Agent_3 rejected every such proposal. Round 2 voting was unanimous across all three proposals because each proposal implemented the same balanced allocation.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflection entries explicitly diagnose the Round 1 blocker as the absence of a proposal satisfying all top-priority bundles, especially Agent_3's Apple+Pencil. They recommend the Round 2 allocation that ultimately equalized outcomes.

mechanisms.theme: First anchor protected Jewel but did not create a payoff edge
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's first public move framed Jewel custody as the anchor, and the final allocation did keep Jewel with Agent_1. This is limited evidence of agenda-setting, not evidence that the first position beat the last position.
The agents valued disjoint bundles, so the stable solution gave each agent exactly its high-value items. That structure made equality easy once Agent_3's bundle was respected.

mechanisms.theme: Agent_3's veto blocked the first round
mechanisms.explanation: The first-position proposal was not enough to win. It left Agent_3 empty, and Agent_3 rejected proposals that failed to include Apple or Pencil, preventing unanimity in Round 1.

evidence.quote: Proposal 1 allocates no items to Agent_3.

mechanisms.theme: Final consensus came from the balanced Round 2 allocation
mechanisms.explanation: All Round 2 proposals converged on the same deal, and every agent accepted. The accepted outcome awarded the last-position agent its top pair and produced equal final utilities, so the last position was not beaten.
